title: Build the Model
subtitle: Turn your idea into numbers a founder can defend.

gameplay.instructions: Time to think like a CFO. Take your own business idea (or ChocoNation's mango bar launch) and sketch its first-year money story in plain words and rough numbers. You don't need a spreadsheet — you need LOGIC: where money comes from, what each sale costs, what running the business costs, and what's left. As a worked example: ChocoNation sells bars at ₹30 that cost ₹18 to make, and spends about ₹25,000 a month on rent, delivery and ads — so it needs roughly 2,100 bars a month just to cover overheads before earning a rupee of profit.

fields.label: Revenue
fields.prompt: What do you sell, at what price, and roughly how many units a month? Show your monthly revenue estimate and say why that volume is believable.
fields.placeholder: We sell X at ₹Y to..., about N per month because...

fields.label: Direct Costs
fields.prompt: What does ONE unit cost you to make or deliver (materials, packaging, delivery per unit)? What's your margin per unit after these costs?
fields.placeholder: Each unit costs ₹Z: ... so we keep ₹... per sale

fields.label: Overheads (SG&A)
fields.prompt: What does it cost to run the business each month regardless of sales — rent, salaries, marketing, tools? Give a monthly total with a rough breakup.

fields.label: Profit (EBITDA)
fields.prompt: Put it together: monthly revenue minus direct costs minus overheads. Is the result positive or negative — and how many units per month would you need to break even?

rubric.label: Numbers connect
rubric.description: The four fields form one consistent story: the EBITDA line actually follows from the revenue, direct cost, and overhead numbers given, with arithmetic roughly right.

rubric.label: Believable assumptions
rubric.description: Prices, volumes, and costs are plausible for the idea described, and at least one assumption is justified (why N units, why that price) rather than asserted.

rubric.label: Costs in the right buckets
rubric.description: Per-unit costs sit in Direct Costs and fixed running costs sit in Overheads — nothing important is double-counted or misplaced.

rubric.label: Understands break-even
rubric.description: States whether the business makes or loses money monthly and shows awareness of what would have to change (units, price, or costs) to break even.
